title: Vaccines & Immunology — NAPLEX / PTCE
description: ACIP immunization schedules, vaccine types, storage requirements, contraindications, and immune modulating drugs for pharmacists.
subject: pharmacy

cards.front: Live attenuated vaccines: examples and key contraindication
cards.back: MMR, Varicella, Zoster (Zostavax), LAIV (FluMist), Yellow Fever, Oral Typhoid, BCG, Rotavirus. Contraindication: immunocompromised patients (HIV with CD4 < 200, active chemotherapy, high-dose steroids), pregnancy (except Yellow Fever if travel risk exceeds risk)

cards.front: Inactivated vaccines: examples and key advantage
cards.back: Inactivated influenza (IIV), IPV, Hepatitis A, Hepatitis B, HPV, Pneumococcal (PPSV23), Meningococcal, Tdap/Td, Rabies, Japanese encephalitis, Shingrix (adjuvanted). Can be given to immunocompromised patients safely (may have reduced response)

cards.front: mRNA vaccines: mechanism and examples
cards.back: mRNA encodes antigen → taken up by cells → ribosomes produce antigen → immune response. COVID-19 vaccines: Pfizer-BioNTech (BNT162b2), Moderna (mRNA-1273). Advantages: rapid development, strong immune response. Does not integrate into host DNA

cards.front: Influenza vaccine: who needs it annually
cards.back: ACIP: everyone ≥ 6 months annually. High-risk: age ≥ 65, pregnancy, chronic disease (asthma, COPD, DM, heart disease, CKD), immunocompromised, healthcare workers, residents of long-term care facilities. LAIV (FluMist): 2–49 yr, not pregnant, not immunocompromised

cards.front: High-dose influenza vaccine (Fluzone HD) and adjuvanted (FLUAD): indication
cards.back: Recommended for adults ≥ 65 years. Fluzone HD: 4× standard antigen dose. FLUAD: standard dose with MF59 adjuvant. Both generate stronger immune response in elderly (immunosenescence). Either is preferred over standard dose IIV in ≥ 65 group (ACIP, 2022)

cards.front: Pneumococcal vaccines: PCV15, PCV20, PPSV23 — ACIP schedule
cards.back: Adults ≥ 65 or high-risk younger adults: PCV20 alone (preferred) OR PCV15 → PPSV23 (≥ 1 year later). Previously vaccinated with PPSV23 only: give PCV20 or PCV15 ≥ 1 year after last pneumococcal vaccine. Never repeat PPSV23 within 5 years in adults < 65

cards.front: Herpes zoster vaccine (Shingrix): schedule and advantages over Zostavax
cards.back: Shingrix (RZV): recombinant adjuvanted — 2 doses 2–6 months apart (may use 1–2 months if immunocompromised). Recommended ≥ 50 yr. Efficacy: ~97% (vs 51% for Zostavax). Can give to immunocompromised (unlike Zostavax). Zostavax discontinued in US (2020)

cards.front: HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9): schedule and age recommendations
cards.back: Routine: 11–12 yr (can start at 9); 2-dose series if started before 15th birthday (0, 6–12 months). 3-dose if started at 15 or older (0, 1–2, 6 months). Catch-up through age 26 (shared decision-making 27–45). Prevents HPV types 6,11,16,18,31,33,45,52,58

cards.front: Meningococcal vaccines: MenACWY vs MenB — schedule
cards.back: MenACWY (Menactra/Menveo/MenQuadfi): routine at 11–12 yr + booster at 16 yr; high-risk asplenic/complement deficiency patients need revaccination q5 yr. MenB (Bexsero/Trumenba): 16–23 yr (shared decision), all asplenic/complement-deficient; 2-dose series

cards.front: Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP): vaccine schedule
cards.back: Unvaccinated: RIG (20 IU/kg — inject all around wound, remainder IM) + vaccine on days 0, 3, 7, 14. Previously vaccinated: vaccine only on days 0 and 3 (no RIG). Timing: as soon as possible after exposure. Rabies immune globulin provides passive immediate immunity

cards.front: Td vs Tdap: difference and current ACIP recommendation
cards.back: Tdap: tetanus + diphtheria + acellular pertussis. Td: tetanus + diphtheria (no pertussis). ACIP: substitute Tdap once as adult booster, then Td or Tdap q10 yr. Pregnancy: Tdap every pregnancy (27–36 wk), regardless of prior Tdap history — protects newborn via maternal antibodies

cards.front: Vaccine storage temperature requirements: 'Vaccines for Children' cold chain
cards.back: Refrigerated vaccines: 2–8°C (36–46°F). Frozen vaccines: −50 to −15°C. Varicella/Zoster/MMRV: frozen. mRNA COVID vaccines (Moderna): −25 to −15°C or refrigerated short-term. Never refreeze thawed vaccines. Temperature excursions must be reported and handled per manufacturer

cards.front: Egg allergy and influenza/yellow fever vaccines: current guidance
cards.back: Egg allergy: most patients including egg allergy can receive IIV — monitor 15 min post-vaccination. Severe egg allergy (anaphylaxis): give in medical setting with 30 min observation OR use Flucelvax/Flublok (cell-based, recombinant — egg-free). Yellow fever: avoid or consult allergist

cards.front: Vaccinations contraindicated in pregnancy (live vaccines)
cards.back: Contraindicated: MMR, Varicella, Live-attenuated influenza (FluMist), Yellow Fever (avoid if possible). Give MMR and Varicella before conception or postpartum. Inactivated and subunit vaccines (IIV, Tdap, Hepatitis B, COVID-19 mRNA) are safe and recommended in pregnancy

cards.front: Immunoglobulin (IG) effect on live vaccines
cards.back: Blood products/IG contain antibodies that can neutralize live vaccines. Wait 3–11 months after IG before giving MMR or Varicella (depending on IG dose). If live vaccine given first, wait ≥ 2 weeks before IG. Inactivated vaccines unaffected

cards.front: Simultaneous vaccine administration: general principle
cards.back: ACIP: multiple vaccines may be given at same visit (different sites, different syringes). No evidence of reduced efficacy or increased adverse effects. Exception: never give two live injectable vaccines at same visit unless they're in a combo product (MMRV) — or give at same visit; otherwise wait 28 days

cards.front: Childhood vaccination schedule: first birthday vaccines
cards.back: At 12–15 months: MMR, Varicella, Hib, PCV15/PCV20, Hepatitis A (1 of 2). 12–23 months: Hepatitis A series. After 12 months: hepatitis A and varicella can begin (not before 12 months). Rotavirus: completed by 8 months (do not initiate after 14 weeks)

cards.front: Rotavirus vaccine: schedule and important limitation
cards.back: Rotarix (RV1): 2 doses at 2 and 4 months. RotaTeq (RV5): 3 doses at 2, 4, 6 months. DO NOT start if ≥ 15 weeks 0 days old (risk of intussusception). Complete before 8 months. History of intussusception: contraindication. SCID: contraindication

cards.front: Immunocompromised patient: which vaccines to avoid and which are safe
cards.back: Avoid (live vaccines): MMR, Varicella, Zostavax, LAIV, Yellow Fever, Oral Typhoid. Safe (inactivated/recombinant): IIV, Shingrix, Pneumococcal, Meningococcal, HPV, Tdap, Hepatitis A, Hepatitis B, COVID-19 (mRNA and protein subunit). May have reduced immune response

cards.front: Passive vs active immunization: distinction
cards.back: Active: vaccine stimulates host immune system → produces antibodies + memory cells → long-lasting protection. Passive: pre-formed antibodies given (immune globulin, monoclonal antibodies) → immediate but temporary protection (weeks to months). Both used in post-exposure prophylaxis (e.g., hepatitis B, rabies)

cards.front: Hepatitis B: vaccine schedule and post-exposure prophylaxis
cards.back: Adult: 3-dose series (0, 1, 6 months). Heplisav-B: 2 doses 1 month apart (adults ≥ 18). Unvaccinated exposed to HBV: HBIG + vaccine series. Known responder: no action. Known non-responder: HBIG × 2 doses 1 month apart. Screen HBsAg-positive individuals for treatment

cards.front: Hepatitis A vaccine: schedule and post-exposure prophylaxis
cards.back: 2-dose series: 0 and 6–18 months. Post-exposure: vaccine OR IG within 2 weeks. Vaccine preferred for healthy 1–40 yr. IG preferred if < 1 yr, > 40 yr, immunocompromised, chronic liver disease, vaccine contraindicated. IG: 0.1 mL/kg IM

cards.front: Varicella: vaccine schedule and contraindications
cards.back: 2-dose series: 12–15 months + 4–6 years. Catch-up for unvaccinated: 2 doses ≥ 4 weeks apart. Contraindications: immunocompromised, pregnancy, history of anaphylaxis to neomycin. Avoid salicylates for 6 weeks post-vaccination (Reye's syndrome risk)

cards.front: MMRV (ProQuad): when to use and when to avoid
cards.back: MMRV: MMR + Varicella combo. Can use at 12–47 months but slightly ↑ febrile seizure risk at 12–23 months (1:2,300 vs 1:4,600 with separate injections). ACIP prefers separate MMR + Varicella at 12–15 months first dose. MMRV acceptable for second dose at 4–6 years

cards.front: Tdap in pregnancy: mechanism of protection for newborn
cards.back: Tdap at 27–36 weeks gestation → maternal IgG crosses placenta → newborn protected against pertussis in first months of life before own vaccine series. 'Cocooning' (vaccinating close contacts) is less effective than maternal vaccination. Give Tdap each pregnancy regardless of prior Tdap

cards.front: Adjuvants in vaccines: purpose and examples
cards.back: Adjuvants enhance immunogenicity (allow lower antigen dose, stronger and longer-lasting response). Examples: aluminum salts (alum) — Hepatitis B, Tdap; MF59 oil-in-water emulsion — FLUAD; AS01B — Shingrix (MPL + QS-21); AS04 — Cervarix. mRNA vaccines use lipid nanoparticles as delivery systems (also adjuvant-like properties)

cards.front: Herd immunity threshold: formula and examples
cards.back: Threshold = 1 − (1/R0). R0 is basic reproduction number. Measles R0 ≈ 12–18 → need ~92–95% vaccinated. Polio R0 ≈ 5–7 → need ~80–85%. COVID-19 original strain R0 ≈ 2–3; Delta/Omicron R0 much higher. Higher transmissibility requires higher vaccination coverage for herd protection

cards.front: Anaphylaxis management post-vaccination: first-line treatment
cards.back: Epinephrine IM 0.3–0.5 mg (1:1000 solution, 0.3–0.5 mL) in anterolateral thigh — FIRST LINE. Repeat q5–15 min if needed. Call 911. Adjuncts: H1/H2 antihistamines (diphenhydramine, famotidine), corticosteroids (reduce delayed reactions). All vaccination sites must have epinephrine available

cards.front: Immunotherapy (allergy shots): mechanism
cards.back: Subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT) desensitizes via: Th2 → Th1 shift, induction of regulatory T cells, ↑ IgG4 (blocking antibody), gradual ↓ IgE-mediated response. Sublingual (SLIT) also available. 3–5 year course. Risk: anaphylaxis — observe 20–30 min post-injection

cards.front: Immunosuppressants used in transplant: mechanism overview
cards.back: Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine): block IL-2 production → suppress T-cell activation. mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus): block T-cell proliferation. Antimetabolites (mycophenolate, azathioprine): inhibit purine synthesis. Corticosteroids: broad anti-inflammatory

cards.front: Biologics targeting TNF-α: drugs and indications
cards.back: Infliximab (Remicade), Adalimumab (Humira), Etanercept (Enbrel), Certolizumab (Cimzia), Golimumab (Simponi). Indications: RA, Crohn's, UC, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis. Screen for TB before starting (reactivation risk). CI: active serious infection, CHF (NYHA III-IV), active malignancy

cards.front: Rituximab: mechanism, indications, monitoring
cards.back: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody → B cell depletion. Indications: NHL, CLL, RA, autoimmune (pemphigus vulgaris, granulomatosis with polyangiitis). Monitor: CBC (neutropenia), hepatitis B reactivation (screen before use), PML (JC virus — rare, fatal). Pre-medicate: methylprednisolone + antihistamine

cards.front: Checkpoint inhibitors (immune-related adverse events)
cards.back: PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) and CTLA-4 (ipilimumab) inhibitors: unleash T-cell anti-tumor immunity but cause immune-related adverse events (irAEs): colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies (hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency), dermatitis. Treatment: high-dose corticosteroids; hold/stop immunotherapy for severe irAEs

cards.front: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG): uses and mechanisms
cards.back: IVIG contains pooled IgG from thousands of donors. Uses: primary immunodeficiency (replacement), ITP (blocks Fc receptors on macrophages → ↓ platelet destruction), Kawasaki disease, Guillain-Barré, myasthenia gravis crisis. Adverse effects: headache, renal failure (sucrose formulations), aseptic meningitis, hemolysis

cards.front: Pneumococcal vaccine timing after splenectomy
cards.back: Elective splenectomy: give PCV20 (or PCV15 → PPSV23) ≥ 2 weeks BEFORE surgery to maximize immune response. Emergency splenectomy: vaccinate ≥ 2 weeks post-op (immune response is blunted earlier). Revaccinate with PPSV23 5 years later (if high risk). Lifelong penicillin prophylaxis for some patients

cards.front: BCG vaccine: who gets it and what it protects against
cards.back: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin): live attenuated Mycobacterium bovis. Given at birth in countries with high TB prevalence. Protects against severe TB in children (miliary TB, TB meningitis). NOT routinely recommended in US. May cause false-positive TST/PPD. Does NOT reliably prevent pulmonary TB

cards.front: Oral typhoid vaccine (Vivotif): regimen and contraindications
cards.back: Live attenuated Ty21a. Regimen: 1 capsule every other day × 4 doses; start ≥ 2 weeks before travel. Refrigerate capsules. Take with cool water ≥ 1 hr before meals. Complete course ≥ 1 week before travel. CI: immunocompromised, age < 6 yr. Antibiotics: avoid for 3 days before/during/after course

cards.front: Travel vaccines and pre-travel considerations
cards.back: Assess destination risks, trip duration, activities, health status. Common travel vaccines: Hepatitis A, Typhoid (oral or IM), Yellow Fever (certificate required for some countries), Japanese Encephalitis, Meningococcal, Rabies (pre-exposure if high risk). Antimalarials: begin before departure

cards.front: Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS)
cards.back: Passive surveillance system: anyone can report (patients, providers, manufacturers). Tracks safety signals but does NOT prove causation. Serious adverse events after licensed vaccines MUST be reported by healthcare providers. Vaccine Injury Compensation Program (VICP) provides legal compensation for injuries

cards.front: COVID-19 vaccine: current ACIP recommendations (2024)
cards.back: Updated (2023–2024 formula) COVID-19 vaccine recommended annually for all ≥ 6 months. Unvaccinated: 2+ doses to complete primary series (brand-dependent) + updated booster. Immunocompromised: additional doses (up to 3 primary doses + booster). mRNA vaccines preferred over J&J (withdrawn US)

cards.front: Varicella-zoster immunoglobulin (VariZIG): use
cards.back: Post-exposure prophylaxis for high-risk individuals exposed to varicella (VZV) who cannot receive live vaccine. High-risk: immunocompromised, pregnant women, newborns of mothers with varicella near delivery. Give within 10 days of exposure. Alternative: acyclovir prophylaxis (7 days, starting 7–10 days after exposure)

cards.front: Pharmacist's role in vaccination: scope of practice
cards.back: All 50 states allow pharmacists to administer vaccines. Scope includes: patient assessment, counseling, administration, documentation in IIS/immunization registry, adverse event management. ACIP recommendations guide vaccine selection. Standing orders permit vaccination without individual physician prescription

cards.front: Live vaccines and immunosuppressive therapy: timing
cards.back: Before starting immunosuppressants: administer all needed live vaccines ≥ 4 weeks before starting. After stopping immunosuppressants: wait ≥ 3 months before giving live vaccines (time needed for immune reconstitution). Biologic timing varies: generally wait ≥ 5 half-lives of the biologic

cards.front: Zoster (shingles): who should NOT receive Shingrix
cards.back: Do not give Shingrix to: those with current shingles outbreak (defer until resolved), documented immunity to herpes zoster disease unnecessary but not harmful, those with severe allergy to any component. Shingrix IS safe for immunocompromised patients (unlike Zostavax). No minimum age restriction below 50 for immunocompromised

cards.front: Immunization Information Systems (IIS): pharmacist role
cards.back: IIS (immunization registries): centralized database of patient vaccination records across providers. Pharmacists should: query IIS before vaccinating (avoid duplicate doses), enter administered vaccines into IIS, generate reminder/recall notices. Participation is required in many states. Bidirectional exchange supported by HL7/FHIR

cards.front: Prevnar vs Vaxneuvance vs Pneumovax: differences
cards.back: PCV20 (Prevnar 20): 20-valent pneumococcal conjugate. PCV15 (Vaxneuvance): 15-valent conjugate. PPSV23 (Pneumovax 23): 23-valent polysaccharide (not conjugated). Conjugate vaccines (PCV): generate T-cell-dependent response, immunologic memory, effective in children. PPSV23: T-cell independent, no memory, less effective in young children

cards.front: Influenza vaccine efficacy: why it varies annually
cards.back: Influenza viruses undergo antigenic drift (gradual mutations) and antigenic shift (novel reassortment). Vaccine is reformulated annually based on WHO surveillance. Efficacy: typically 40–60% against influenza A/B when well-matched. Even partial mismatch provides benefit: less severe illness, fewer hospitalizations

cards.front: Monoclonal antibody passive immunization: example for RSV
cards.back: Nirsevimab (Beyfortus): anti-RSV monoclonal antibody. Give to all infants ≤ 8 months entering first RSV season (typically Oct–Mar) and high-risk children up to 24 months. Palivizumab: older monoclonal for high-risk infants (preterm, CHD, pulmonary disease). These provide passive immunity without active vaccination
